summary.overall_assessment: The artifact is well-structured and covers all major sections. However, it contains three must-fix contradictions that will cause implementation failure: (1) the €2K-5K/month budget is mathematically incompatible with 99.95% SLA and 200 concurrent AI agents at production scale; (2) GDPR Article 17 right-to-erasure cannot be satisfied with an event-streaming architecture using Event Hubs since individual records cannot be selectively deleted; (3) multi-tenant hospital data isolation is classified as 'Should' priority despite being a hard GDPR legal requirement.

findings.title: Budget (€2K-5K/month) is irreconcilable with 99.95% SLA and 200 concurrent AI agents
findings.description: The requirements simultaneously demand: (a) 99.95% SLA requiring zone-redundant deployments of every service, (b) 200 concurrent agent executions, (c) APIM which requires Standard v2 tier at ~€700/month for any contractual SLA above 99.9%, (d) AI Foundry inference costs that at 10,000 executions/day at conservative €0.05/execution = €1,500/day. Zone-redundant Container Apps need minimum 3 replicas per zone. The compound SLA of 10 services in a chain will be lower than the weakest link.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) raise the budget ceiling to €10K-20K/month to match the SLA and concurrency requirements, or (b) reduce SLA target to 99.9% (removes zone-redundancy requirement), or (c) scope 200 concurrent agents to dev/staging tiers only for MVP and define a per-hospital cost model that transfers AI inference costs to hospital contracts.

findings.title: GDPR Article 17 Right to Erasure is architecturally unimplementable with Event Hubs
findings.description: The requirements mandate GDPR Article 17 compliance (data deletion on request) while simultaneously routing patient records and clinical scheduling data through Azure Event Hubs. Event Hubs does not support selective message deletion — data is retained for the full retention window (7 days stated) and cannot be targeted by patient ID for deletion.
findings.recommendation: Define an explicit data flow classification: distinguish between (a) operational metadata (safe to stream — patient ID, timestamps, event types) and (b) PII/PHI payload (must never enter Event Hubs in raw form). Require that PHI is stored in an addressable store (Cosmos DB or SQL) with pointer-based events in Event Hubs.

findings.title: Multi-tenant hospital data isolation classified as 'Should' but is a hard GDPR legal requirement
findings.description: Functional Requirement #7 (Multi-tenant Isolation) is marked 🟡 Should priority, implying it can be descoped for MVP. However, for a platform processing patient records across 25 different hospitals, GDPR Article 5(1)(f) (integrity and confidentiality) and the NEN 7510 standard both mandate strict logical separation so Hospital A cannot access Hospital B's patient data.
findings.recommendation: Change the priority of Functional Requirement #7 to 🔴 Must and add an acceptance criterion: 'Cross-hospital data access is impossible at the data layer (not just application layer)'. Document whether tenant isolation will be achieved via separate resource groups per hospital, shared infrastructure with row-level security, or namespace-per-tenant.

findings.title: DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) not listed as a compliance requirement
findings.description: GDPR Article 35 mandates a Data Protection Impact Assessment before processing 'large scale' special category data (health data). Processing patient records for 25 hospitals qualifies unambiguously.
findings.recommendation: Add a compliance requirement: 'DPIA must be conducted and approved by the Data Protection Officer (DPO) before production go-live'. List DPIA findings as a dependency for the architecture step.

findings.title: No Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with Microsoft Azure referenced
findings.description: GDPR Article 28 requires a written Data Processing Agreement with every processor who handles personal data on the controller's behalf. Microsoft provides a standard DPA via Azure's Data Protection Addendum.
findings.recommendation: Add a compliance requirement: 'Microsoft Azure Data Protection Addendum (DPA) must be reviewed and accepted before any patient data is ingested into Azure services'.

findings.title: RTO of 4 hours mathematically contradicts 99.95% SLA
findings.description: 99.95% SLA allows 4.38 hours of downtime per year. The stated RTO is 4 hours. This means a single full-RTO incident would consume 91% of the entire annual SLA budget, leaving only 22 minutes of tolerance for all other outages across the year.
findings.recommendation: Choose one of: (a) Reduce SLA target to 99.9% (8.76 hours/year allowance, compatible with 4h RTO), or (b) Reduce RTO to ≤1 hour to leave headroom within the 99.95% budget.

findings.title: WAF (Web Application Firewall) excluded for MVP on public-facing APIM carrying healthcare data
findings.description: The requirements explicitly state 'WAF required: ❌ Not required for MVP'. However, the public APIM endpoint is the entry point for 25 hospitals transmitting clinical and patient data. Without a WAF, the platform is exposed to OWASP Top 10 attacks on healthcare-sensitive APIs.
findings.recommendation: Add Azure Front Door with WAF (Prevention mode) in front of APIM, or enable the Azure API Management built-in WAF policy set. Front Door WAF at Standard tier is ~€50-150/month which is within the budget envelope.

findings.title: NEN 7510 breach notification and incident response plan not referenced
findings.description: NEN 7510 (and GDPR Article 33) require notification to the Dutch Data Protection Authority within 72 hours of a personal data breach. The operational requirements define support hours as 'business hours for MVP' with no 24/7 on-call.
findings.recommendation: Add an operational requirement: 'Security incident detection must be automated (via Azure Sentinel or Defender for Cloud alerts) with on-call notification for data breach indicators, regardless of MVP support hours'.

findings.title: Customer-Managed Keys (CMK) deferred to Phase 2 despite NEN 7510 encryption requirements
findings.description: Platform-managed keys mean Microsoft controls the encryption keys. Dutch healthcare regulators have been increasingly requiring organizations to retain control of encryption keys for PHI.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit accepted risk entry: 'Platform-managed encryption keys for MVP are an accepted risk pending legal review. If Dutch healthcare data authority or hospital procurement requirements mandate CMK, this will be an MVP blocker.'
findings.section: 🔒 Compliance & Security Requirements / Recommended Security Controls

findings.title: No CI/CD pipeline or deployment automation requirements defined
findings.description: The operational requirements cover monitoring, support, backup and DR, but contain no requirements for the deployment pipeline itself. For a 7-person team deploying to a complex multi-service healthcare platform, this is a significant gap.
findings.recommendation: Add a requirement: 'Fully automated CI/CD pipeline required (GitHub Actions) with separate Dev and Prod environments, mandatory IaC validation (bicep lint + what-if) before promotion'.

findings.title: AI Foundry quota risk acknowledged but no quota pre-verification requirement defined
findings.description: Azure AI Foundry (particularly GPT-4o) has per-region TPM quotas that can have months-long waitlists. For 200 concurrent agents with p95 < 30s response time, substantial TPM quota is required.
findings.recommendation: Add a pre-architecture prerequisite: 'Verify AI Foundry model availability and quota capacity in swedencentral before architecture step.'
findings.section: 📋 Summary for Architecture Assessment / Open Risks

findings.title: Hospital Dashboards outbound SLA (99.95%) leaves no downstream buffer
findings.description: The integrations table shows Hospital Dashboards (outbound) with a 99.95% SLA requirement, which exactly equals the platform's own SLA target — zero tolerance margin for cascading failures.
findings.recommendation: Either lower the hospital dashboard SLA expectation to 99.9%, or state that the 99.95% platform SLA is measured at the APIM gateway level with graceful degradation for individual backend failures.

findings.title: Azure Budget alert resource requirement not stated
findings.description: The budget section defines €2K-5K/month but there is no requirement to implement Azure Budget alerts. For a startup with Series A funding, cost overruns from unexpected AI inference usage could be significant.
findings.recommendation: Add an operational requirement: 'Azure Budget resource must be deployed via IaC with alerts at 80%, 100%, and 120% of monthly budget ceiling.'
